definition: High-level policy should depend on abstractions, not on low-level implementation details.
ruleOfThumb: Business rules should describe what they need, not who specifically provides it.

citation.author: Robert C. Martin
citation.bookTitle: Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

questions.prompt: What coupling issue makes this service hard to evolve?

options.text: The business flow directly constructs a specific mailer, so changing delivery provider or testing behavior requires editing this high-level service
options.explanation: SignupService owns policy-level behavior, but it is tied to one concrete transport. It should depend on a notification abstraction and receive an implementation externally.

options.text: Email should be sent via an async queue so failures don't block the registration response or retry automatically
options.explanation: Queues may be useful operationally, but they do not address the dependency direction problem shown.

options.text: The smtp field should be readonly so other methods cannot reassign it and introduce unpredictable mailer behavior
options.explanation: Readonly can improve safety, but it does not decouple policy from implementation.

options.text: The method should return a boolean so callers can react to whether email delivery succeeded or failed
options.explanation: Return shape can be discussed, but the architectural issue is concrete dependency wiring inside high-level code.

questions.prompt: Why does this reporting use case become brittle when storage technology changes?

options.text: The use case directly owns database construction and query details, so persistence changes force edits in business logic code
options.explanation: High-level report policy should not know SQL client setup or vendor details. A repository abstraction keeps policy stable while storage implementations vary.

options.text: Using SQL text inside run() is always wrong; all SQL should live in stored procedures
options.explanation: Stored procedures are an option, not a requirement. The core problem is dependency direction, not SQL location.

options.text: The toReport conversion helper should be static since it doesn't use instance state and can be called more efficiently
options.explanation: Static conversion does not solve the tight coupling between business policy and concrete data source.

options.text: The type unknown[] indicates missing generic constraints and is the main source of brittleness
options.explanation: Typing can improve correctness, but the bigger maintainability risk is hard-coding low-level dependencies in high-level code.

questions.prompt: The team plans to support SMS and push alerts in addition to email. Which version keeps business rules most insulated from those changes?

options.text: Version A — introducing an interface adds indirection that makes the design less maintainable over time
options.explanation: The added abstraction is purposeful: it isolates high-level policy from implementation churn, which improves maintainability.

options.text: Version B — policy code is tied to one concrete channel, so each new transport requires reopening this use case
options.explanation: B must be modified for SMS, push, retries, and tests. In A, the use case stays stable while channel implementations vary behind an abstraction.
